name: Walvis Bay
summary: Walvis Bay is a sunny port town in Namibia where the world's oldest desert meets the cold Atlantic Ocean. Thousands of pink flamingos wade in its huge salty lagoon while giant orange sand dunes rise just behind the town.
funFacts: More than 100,000 birds visit Walvis Bay's lagoon each year, and most of southern Africa's pink flamingos come here to feed in the salty water.
The Namib Desert next door is probably the oldest desert on Earth, baking under the sun for around 55 to 80 million years.
At Sandwich Harbour just south of town, some of the planet's tallest sand dunes tumble straight down into the crashing Atlantic waves.
Thick fog rolls in off the cold ocean over 180 days a year, and clever beetles do headstands so droplets slide into their mouths.
Dune 7 stands about 132 metres tall and is one of the highest sand dunes in the world that you can climb.
thingsToDo: Walk along Walvis Bay Lagoon at sunrise to spot thousands of pink flamingos wading in the shallow water.
Hop on a catamaran cruise to meet curious dolphins and friendly Cape fur seals near Pelican Point lighthouse.
Take a 4x4 trip to Sandwich Harbour where giant dunes drop straight down into the cold Atlantic Ocean.
Climb Dune 7, one of the tallest sand dunes in the world, and slide back down on a sandboard.
Drive 30 kilometres up the coast to Swakopmund to wander its old German streets and seaside aquarium.
Search the Welwitschia Plains for the strange two-leaved desert plant that has been alive since Roman times.

wildlife.name: Lesser Flamingo
wildlife.description: Bright pink wading bird that filters tiny food from salty lagoon water with its curvy beak.

wildlife.name: Greater Flamingo
wildlife.description: Tall pale-pink flamingo with very long pink legs that wades in shallow water beside its smaller cousin.

wildlife.name: Cape Fur Seal
wildlife.description: Whiskered seal that barks loudly and lazes in big noisy colonies along the rocky Atlantic shore.

wildlife.name: Great White Pelican
wildlife.description: Huge white waterbird with a giant stretchy throat pouch it uses to scoop up fish.

localWord.meaning: Hello in Afrikaans, one of Namibia's main languages.

images.alt: Giant sand dunes tumble right into the blue Atlantic Ocean at Sandwich Harbour near Walvis Bay.
An aerial view of Walvis Bay, its lagoon and the surrounding Namib Desert from space.
Dune 7, a huge orange sand dune near Walvis Bay, with people climbing its steep ridge.
The red-and-white Pelican Point lighthouse on a sandy spit at Walvis Bay.
The red-and-white striped Swakopmund lighthouse seen from above with the colourful seaside town around it.
A funny-looking ancient Welwitschia plant with long curly leaves growing in the Namib Desert.
A big flock of bright pink greater flamingos wading in the shallow lagoon at Walvis Bay.
A close-up of a bright pink lesser flamingo standing in shallow water.
Hundreds of Cape fur seals barking and lazing on the rocky Namibian shoreline.
A great white pelican floating on the water at Walvis Bay with its big yellow throat pouch.
